title: Why 43% of Physiotherapy Care Is Non-Recommended — and What Clinics Can Do About It
meta_description: Nearly half of physiotherapy care doesn't follow evidence-based guidelines. Learn why this happens and proven strategies to improve clinical consistency.
html_content:
Recent audit data reveals a troubling reality: up to 43% of physiotherapy interventions delivered in UK MSK practices don't align with current clinical guidelines. This isn't about individual competence — it's about systematic gaps in how practices translate research into consistent care delivery.

For clinic owners and senior clinicians, this represents both a quality problem and a business risk. Non-recommended care extends treatment duration, increases patient dropout rates, and creates liability exposure that traditional insurance models may not cover. More fundamentally, it undermines the clinical confidence that drives patient outcomes and practice sustainability.

The Evidence Gap in Routine Practice

The disconnect between research and practice manifests differently across conditions, but the pattern is consistent. In post-surgical ACL rehabilitation, systematic reviews show clear benefit for early open kinetic chain exercises, yet many clinicians delay these interventions based on outdated protocols (Fontanier et al., 2025). Similarly, blood flow restriction training demonstrates superior outcomes compared to standard rehabilitation for ACL recovery, but adoption remains limited despite strong evidence (Bandara et al., 2021).

This gap isn't unique to complex surgical cases. For common conditions like chronic shoulder pain, digital physiotherapy platforms have demonstrated non-inferiority to conventional face-to-face treatment in randomised trials (Pak et al., 2023). Yet most practices continue resource-intensive traditional delivery models without exploring hybrid approaches that could improve access while maintaining outcomes.

The underlying issue is that individual clinical experience — however extensive — cannot compete with the statistical power of systematic reviews and meta-analyses. When clinicians rely primarily on subjective assessment and personal experience, practice variation inevitably increases, and some patients receive interventions that research has shown to be less effective.

Why Guidelines Don't Translate to Practice

Three structural factors explain why evidence-based protocols often fail to reach patients. First, clinical guidelines typically present multiple intervention options without clear decision frameworks. A systematic review might conclude that "exercise therapy is effective for rotator cuff repair rehabilitation" without specifying which exercises, at what intensity, or for which patient subgroups (Bandara et al., 2021).

Second, most physiotherapy practices lack objective measurement systems to track whether interventions are working. Without consistent outcome data, clinicians cannot distinguish between effective and ineffective approaches in their own caseloads. This creates a feedback loop where practitioners continue using familiar techniques regardless of their comparative effectiveness.

Third, time pressures incentivise treatment approaches that feel efficient rather than approaches that research shows to be effective. A clinician might choose manual therapy because it fills a 30-minute slot, even when exercise prescription would deliver better long-term outcomes but requires more complex progression monitoring.

The Cost of Clinical Variation

Non-recommended care generates costs that extend beyond individual patient outcomes. Treatment protocols that don't align with current evidence typically require more sessions to achieve functional goals. This increases direct costs per episode while reducing clinic capacity for new patients.

More critically, inconsistent care creates reputational risks that are difficult to quantify but expensive to resolve. When patients receive different treatment approaches for similar presentations, word-of-mouth referrals become unpredictable. Referring clinicians — GPs, consultants, sports medicine physicians — lose confidence in practice consistency and may redirect referrals to competitors with more standardised protocols.

For practices working with insurance providers or occupational health contracts, clinical variation represents a significant business risk. Payers increasingly demand evidence that treatment protocols align with published guidelines. Practices that cannot demonstrate systematic adherence to recommended care pathways may face contract renegotiation or termination.

Building Systems for Evidence-Based Practice

The solution isn't more continuing education or additional guideline documents. Instead, practices need infrastructure that makes evidence-based decisions the default choice rather than an additional burden. This starts with objective measurement systems that track patient progress using validated, standardised tools.

Research demonstrates that structured rehabilitation protocols produce superior outcomes compared to ad hoc approaches. For meniscus repair rehabilitation, systematic reviews show that evidence-based protocols significantly improve return-to-sport rates and reduce re-injury risk (Harput et al., 2020). The key is translating these protocols into decision trees that busy clinicians can follow consistently.

Practical implementation requires four elements: standardised assessment tools that generate objective baseline data, treatment protocols that specify intervention criteria based on measured parameters, progression frameworks that use objective markers rather than time-based advancement, and outcome tracking that compares individual patient results to established benchmarks.

Consider how this works for a common presentation like chronic subacromial bursitis. Research shows that combined physiotherapy and corticosteroid injection produces superior outcomes compared to either intervention alone (Hsieh et al., 2023). A practice with systematic protocols would use objective shoulder range-of-motion and strength measurements to identify patients likely to benefit from combined treatment, rather than relying on subjective clinical impression.

Measuring What Matters

The most effective practices distinguish between measuring activity and measuring outcomes. Activity metrics — session attendance, treatment duration, patient satisfaction scores — don't predict clinical effectiveness. Outcome metrics — objective strength improvements, functional capacity gains, return-to-activity timelines — directly correlate with evidence-based practice adherence.

For post-surgical rehabilitation like total knee arthroplasty, systematic reviews demonstrate that early physiotherapy intervention significantly improves quality of life, pain outcomes, and range of motion recovery (Aftab et al., 2025). Practices that track these specific parameters can identify when their protocols align with research recommendations and when they need adjustment.

The measurement infrastructure doesn't require expensive equipment. Simple tools — handheld dynamometers, inclinometers, standardised functional tests — generate objective data that guides clinical decisions. The critical factor is consistent use across all clinicians and all similar cases, creating the data foundation that enables evidence-based practice.

Making Evidence-Based Practice Sustainable

Systematic adherence to clinical guidelines requires practice-level commitment, not individual clinician enthusiasm. This means developing protocols that are specific enough to guide decisions but flexible enough to accommodate patient variation. It means training all team members — not just senior clinicians — to use objective assessment tools consistently.

Most importantly, it means recognising that evidence-based practice is not an additional clinical burden but the foundation that makes every other aspect of practice more efficient. When treatment decisions are based on objective data and validated protocols, patient outcomes improve, treatment duration becomes predictable, and clinical confidence increases.

For practices ready to reduce their proportion of non-recommended care, the pathway is straightforward: implement standardised measurement, adopt evidence-based protocols, track outcomes systematically, and use data to refine approaches. The 57% of physiotherapy care that already aligns with guidelines demonstrates that this is achievable with appropriate systems.

Ready to Reduce Clinical Variation?

If your practice wants to align more closely with evidence-based guidelines while improving patient outcomes, Benchmark PS provides the measurement infrastructure and clinical protocols that make systematic care delivery practical. Book a demonstration to see how objective assessment tools can reduce treatment variation and improve clinical decision-making in your practice.

faq.q: What does 'non-recommended care' mean in physiotherapy?
faq.a: Non-recommended care refers to interventions that don't align with current clinical guidelines or evidence-based protocols, often resulting in longer treatment times and suboptimal patient outcomes.

faq.q: How can practices identify if they're delivering non-recommended care?
faq.a: Practices need objective measurement systems to track patient outcomes and compare their results to established benchmarks and evidence-based protocols for similar conditions.

faq.q: What's the main barrier to implementing evidence-based physiotherapy?
faq.a: The primary barrier is lack of systematic measurement and decision-making frameworks that make evidence-based choices the default rather than requiring additional effort from time-pressed clinicians.

original_topic: Why 43% of Physiotherapy Care Is Non-Recommended — and What Clinics Can Do About It
